sentences.text: We-- this is-- this is-- this is when-- when we really-- you know again, you know, we-- again, there was nobody there really that we really knew on this particular boat.
But sort of-- it just happened.
It just happened, particularly with [?
Mono. ?] You know, we started to talk.
And he looked at me as if I were his lifelong sister, that he just meant-- met, after all those years.
And later on, I found out that he lost everybody.
He-- nobody survived, not even a cousin.
Nobody, not even a home, not even a childhood friend.
Yes.
We landed-- we were already close to Haifa, when we got caught.
By the-- by the English soldiers, by the people that occupied Palestine.
We were sent to Cyprus, to Nic-- I think it was in Nicosia.
It was named Nicosia.
And we stayed there till Prince-- when Prince Charles was born-- well over a year.
When Prince Charles was born, this is when we were sort of released.
We were told that we can go.
By that time, Israel-- Palestine was already Israel.
But they still-- one could not really go.
People were-- during this time, some people were escaping, sort of.
They made, underground, certain ways or whatever.
There were always something happening there, as well.
But in something around January, we went to Israel.
And this is when-- when I was in Cyprus, I got sick.
And perhaps because-- again, the body, the pattern, the genes, or whatever-- it responded again in a certain way, that I got, again, like a lockup within the body.
And I needed to have another operation.
And you know, because it was continuous, it was difficult.
And I think, on top of it, what really triggered it off-- it was that I didn't know, but I got an infection whilst going from Germany to-- to Austria.
I somehow caught an infection in the water, or something like this.
And I didn't realize.
So one of the kidneys, you know, was-- sort of wasn't working.
Anyhow, it is really a long story.
But somehow, I was young.
And youth was on my side.
And I still-- there were still-- you know, again, we got-- we became friends.
And there were some people from [?
Bedzin. ?] And we waited for a while.
And we lived in a-- in a [NON-ENGLISH].
We lived like today the most primitive-- [SIGHS] we lived like two families in a [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, like when you go to camping?
Where was?
In-- in Cyprus.
We didn't have barracks or things like that.
We lived in a [NON-ENGLISH].
We had [NON-ENGLISH].
We put down [NON-ENGLISH].
And there were two couples in the [NON-ENGLISH].
Would you believe it?
And we made a home.
I made a home, you know?
I made it look sort of particular, just in a certain way.
It was even-- this is-- if nothing else, this is, you see, what you-- what somehow that you know, that wherever you are, for a-- even for a day, this is a day in your life.
And you make home.
It's your home for this day.
And this is-- this is what it was.
I got-- yes.
It was-- again, it was very primitive.
Because again, you know, it was the same thing.
We didn't have sort of doctors.
And you had to be really a survivor there, whatever.
Anyhow, it happened because I couldn't conceive.
It was-- it still was a throwback, really, to what happened.
You know, I didn't realize till I was talking to you, that this is really-- you know, if I would have known then like what I knew now, you know, I could have sort of-- I don't know what.
But I didn't know.
So-- and life goes on, regardless.
So I sort of tried to eliminate all those you know, feeling sort of-- you know, you have to [? search ?] on.
And it's difficult.
And for a time being, you accept the difficulty.
And then you haven't got a choice.
You are alive.
You can't lie down and die.
You know, so you've got to improve on you-- on the quality of your life.
And improve on your quality on your life-- it comes, again, from within.
You know, it's-- So what happened?
Where did you land in Israel?
Then I landed in Israel.
We-- we landed in [?
Moledet, ?] where, again, somehow-- I don't know how it happened.
But we couldn't-- people were living, again, in such [?
DP ?] sort of place, where people were-- I didn't want to.
I just went out.
I thought, it's a little township.
I knocked.
There was a little-- a little-- one little milk bar.
And this lady was not of Polish descent.
And I didn't know how to talk to her.
And somehow, I talked with my hands, or in Polish, or in Yiddish, or whatever.
And I desperately wanted a little place.
I wanted to be a little wife.
You know, I was in Israel.
And I didn't want to sort of-- to go where there were a lot of people and, again, to go to this kibbutz, you know, to-- I didn't-- so I went to her.
And I said if she's got perhaps something that she can rent us.
You wouldn't believe, as I sit here, she said to me, look.
I got a little house in the back.
We got-- we live where the shop is.
But the little place-- I'm keeping a special place.
But I'll give it to you.
And there was a kitchenette.
And there was a room.
You know, like you have a bungalow or something like this.
And I tell you, that was heaven-sent.
And we stayed there for a while.
It was unbelievable.
We were the only ones.
You know, because people who came in there-- they lived with other people.
Anyhow, we stayed there for a while.
My husband started to work.
Oh, he worked as a plumber, as a interpreter-- you know, whatever he could get.
He was-- he was a very-- put it this way.
If there would be 100 people, and it if would be 10 jobs, he would be the first to get the job.
He went in right way with the notion that he's going to get a job.
And knowing Hebrew fluently, which he did-- so you know, there was-- he got the job straight away.
And he made very good money.
And then I found somebody, again, through somebody-- sort of a relation from a-- some-- something, like further cousin of my-- of my father's.
And he came in, [?
Yakov Zigreich. ?] And he came in.
And I didn't know him before.
And he said, Ruthka, I got this bit of money.
And it is yours.
I already am established a bit.
And he lent us the money.
And we got a little [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH]-- it means that the entrance was the same entrance.
But we had-- on the left-hand side, we had a bedroom and a kitchen.
And the other couple, on the right-hand side, had a bedroom and a kitchen.
That was-- and that was incredible.
And this was new.
And this is what we had.
Altogether, I-- we stayed in Israel 21 months.
Well, I got pregnant.
And I went to the doctor.
As a matter of fact, I got pregnant still in [?
Moledet. ?]
Moledet-- this is the first [INAUDIBLE].
But I went to the doctor there.
And apparently, it was not really-- he did not-- he didn't realize that one-- only one kidney was working, you know.
So he just looked at me.
And I was blooming, you know.
And he kissed my hands.
And I was very young.
And I didn't understand it.
And I was very shy.
And he didn't-- he didn't examine properly, you know.
He didn't really look for something.
But I felt that something-- after the fifth month, I felt that something is not all right.
And it's a long story.
But anyhow, I've-- I just didn't feel the baby.
I was taken to the hospital.
By that time, the baby was already ill.
And my husband was very disappointed.
He was-- he was a-- I was-- at that stage, I was 21.
I was 21?
No, I was maybe not yet.
Something like this.
Anyhow, I was in my very early 20s.
And so he was seven years older.
And so he was fairly young, too.
But somehow, he was very idealistic.
And to him, Israel-- you know, he expected certain happenings.
He expected that all of his longings will be answered there.
And I think that somehow he was very disappointed.
That I think, as lovely as he was, I think he belonged to those people, that faith was a part of him.
But you know, if he got disappointed, he sort took it personally.
Like you know, he should have really changed it, or whatever.
You know, he didn't manage to change it, or things like this, which-- it was very difficult for him.
The food was very scarce, as well.
It was very difficult with the food, you see.
You got portions.
You got just, let's say, for supper, when [INAUDIBLE] for supper, I had a bit of chicken.
And I had a couple of eggs more.
But this is when you go two eggs a week, you know, and when you got all of this.
And so [?
Resha ?] was coming.
Resha ?] was expecting.
And she was coming there.
And she was-- for supper, if I had a bit of chicken, and something, she was carrying it on her body.
It was so precious, you know?
She was covering it up.
When she-- she lived in-- somewhere there in-- not close to Tel Aviv.
And she was coming for the bit of food, you know.
So times were very difficult then, in Israel.
I was sick when I conceived the baby.
I had one kidney working normally.
But because I was very young, and it was not noticeable on me, I looked-- I think I looked even-- I looked sort of-- then I looked very much younger.
And I looked very, sort of-- I have the very fresh skin, and red cheeks.
And I looked like the healthiest human specimen you could imagine.
But in matter of fact, I wasn't well.
And as a matter of fact, something happened, which you might say, you know, it was again a dream.
But I remember, I was in my fifth month.
And twice only my parents-- I dreamt about my parents, really, that I would remember.
And I remember, I had every morning children on the left, and children on the right.
I was always surrounded by children, and with a big stomach.
And I remember the dream that my mother came and took my hand when I was dancing with the children.
And she said look, my darling.
It's time-- it's not-- later on.
You are really not well.
Time is not for you really to have a child now.
It was just as clear as now.
If it is-- if something is-- and I don't know.
It is just what happened.
And I still thought of it, it's just a dream.
You know, I think about her, and whatever.
I don't know if it just a dream or whatever.

But a matter of fact is that I was-- that if it-- if it's something, if there is something, and if it was a warning, then apparently, I didn't-- I did not manage to persuade people in the medical profession that something is not right.
You see, so only-- and then, really, we were going to [?
Kupat ?] [?
Holim, ?] which is-- we were members, like this medical organization, which is [?
Kupat ?] [?
Holim ?] then.
And I came to the doctor.
And I said to him-- that was already in [NON-ENGLISH].
And I said to him, you know, that I feel so heavy.
And somehow I feel that sometimes I have pain here.
So he said, look, this is all pregnancy.
You know, he sort of made a few this.
And he sent me home.
And then that was on-- like, I was going every Friday.
And then that was about Wednesday, or Tuesday-- I suddenly-- I got up in the morning.
And I didn't feel anything.
And that was-- but it was still like the same, like before.
But on Friday, by the time I went on Friday, it was apparently-- it was already obvious to-- to even-- with a-- you could see it with a naked eye, that everything dropped.
So you know, the baby must have been already, you know, for quite a few days.
But it was not detected.
And my husband nearly killed him, the doctor, you know.
He got-- he came in.
And he was outraged.
And I think that this-- having a sister here, and being disappointed, and I was very ill for quite a while.
And there was, again, a doctor, who took care of me.
And if it wouldn't be for this doctor, I wouldn't be allowed to come to Australia.
And this is how we-- you know, it evolved.
For me, I sort of-- as hurt as I was, as disappointed as I was, I think I could separate those two feelings, you know, that Israel was Israel, and people was people.
But for-- for a lot of people, the people from this country, this is what they represent, you know?
So-- So how did you get to Australia?
We came by boat.
Oh.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I really don't remember.
Would you believe that?
I just know we went by boat.
And I think that this was something like we arrived in Melbourne.
We arrived in the dock, somewhere here, with the ships.
When was that?
That was in-- let me think.
That was in '51.
That was in 1951, in June, 1951.
Because in November, '51, I knew that I was pregnant.
So why I came here, I went-- my sister-in-law, Guta, went with me.
I don't know if she remembers it.
Probably she does.
And I was told, in English-- I didn't understand one word of English.
And I was told that in Israel and here-- I know I had a whole sort of things over me, with students, with teachers looking, with doctors looking everywhere.
And I was told, and now you can't conceive.
Everything is blocked.
Everything is in bits and pieces.
It didn't grow back together.
It's not all right.
And you won't-- even if you will conceive, the most you will be able to carry the baby is three months.
As it will grow, it will-- it won't find a place to expand.
Somehow, I didn't believe it.
Somehow, I could not believe it, that after all of this, that-- that something-- whichever way, if I would have to adopt, I probably would adopt.
But I know one thing.
If it wouldn't be for my children, if I survived the war, I would not have been able to live without children.
There is no way.
There is no way.
So I went to Dr.
Meyer.
That was a-- I heard there was here a very good gynecologist, Dr.
Meyer.
And he was then-- he was a convert, as a matter of fact.
He was Jewish who converted to Christianity.
But somehow, again-- I don't know how.
I was just lucky.
He just took to me, you know?
And he just said-- he examined me, and he said, I am not buying this, that you won't have a child.
I'm not buying this.
Something is there.
If it is physical-- whatever it is, you have to do certain things.
I'm going to tell you what you do.
And he told me about conception.
He explained to me certain things-- very simple.
Very-- he said what people-- anyhow, whatever it is, he explained to me.
And things, which in the Jewish religion, when there is a certain time of the month, you-- you-- you know, if you try to conceive, it is really something, which is really a long story.
But I won't-- OK, I want to make it short.
So what happened, that I listened to him.
And you know-- and he was so good to me.
I mean, he was just watching over me, like a-- and somehow, I got pregnant.
But then I got pregnant.
And he said, you see, I told you so.
But now it was-- this what I was told, that I would-- so I was-- this, I couldn't believe.
Because I was told by so many specialists, in Israel and here, that I won't be able to have a child.
So even, no matter what, so it was a most-- it was just as terrible a time during the pregnancy.
Because the fear-- I was in-- I was fearful.
Even when I was asleep, I could reach out and touch that fear.
I was sort of trying to-- you know, sort of-- I was trying to-- to envelop this embryo, when it was in the beginning.
And I went through that third-- and the doctor, Dr.
Meyer, said to me, so I had to go to Switzerland.
And he said, you have to stop eating.
If you have to stop eating-- if the baby will be born very small, you have to stop eating.
So just to eat, just like-- pretend that you are in camp, so just to keep you alive.
You can't-- and he went to Switzerland.
And he said to me, in the meantime, you go to Dr.
Feil.
I will see a Dr.
Feil-- F-E-I-L-- a very famous gynecologist.
And he was really, you know-- he was really something else.
He was really unbelievable.
And I was then end of my fourth month.
And I lost maybe two stone.
You know, I was just a shadow.
He said, look.
You are carrying this baby.
You're not going to have a baby, anyhow, either way.
This is all the aftermath of the war.
This is all-- all of this, please understand, that this is what happened to you.
Your body got such a beating.
And things like this, he went into it.
But you have to-- I was frightened.
He said, you have got to start eating.
Dr.
Meyer-- Dr.
Meyers meant the best-- Meyer meant the best, but you've got to start eating.
So I'd allowed myself a little bit more, a little-- and I still couldn't believe that I'm going to have this baby.
Because you know, you hear those voices of the doctors telling you that you-- it's not possible.
It's not possible.
And there you are.
I gave birth to a boy, who was 7 and 1/2 pounds.
And I tell you what, if anybody is wonderful, he's really incredible, you know?
And he is just-- just incredible.
What's his name?
Julius.
Julius Ray, and now Jules [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
And then I was told that this is it.
It is whatever they call it-- is it a-- whatever it is, OK?
Now, he was six years old.
And I got-- conceived-- I got pregnant with-- which was utterly un-- impossible, unbelievable.
I was told by all the doctors, look.
You can't have another child.
You're going to lose your life now.
And you must-- you must not have another child.
And I said, doctor-- even Dr.
Feil told me this.
And I said to him, look.
I didn't-- I conceived a child after six years.
And if it's meant-- if I-- whatever it meant, it's going to happen.
Whatever it's meant to happen, it will happen.
It's not that I sort of-- you know, that I sort of thought that I'm-- whatever.
I just felt that this is a human life.
And enough of life was destroyed.
I couldn't destroy this human life.
And this human life was born 1 pound and 14 ounces.
And this human life, I was in hospital for months.
And I didn't-- at the end, I didn't feel anything.
Because my blood pressure was such that-- again, she was born.
And nothing-- didn't resemble a baby at all.
And because you could see the top, you know, the-- her forehead was-- I don't know.
It was just-- her hand was like my fingernail.
It was just-- she was just like a-- like a-- you know?
And later on, I was told that I am so lucky that she is normal.
Because for a-- for a long periods, probably, the blood didn't come-- go to her body.
Because I had very high blood-- because of my blood pressure.
So there it is.
What's her name?
Helen [NON-ENGLISH].
She was born in 1959.
Yeah?
My husband-- we both-- in the beginning, he went to work, like he worked in Israel.
But then it wasn't for him.
So he-- we went with another couple.
We bought a milk bar in [?
Deer Park. ?] By that time, I had already Julius.
Because after all, you know, he was born the year after.
He was born in August, '52.
And so we went into the [? milk bar ?] when he was a few months old.
And it just evolved from there.
Then it wasn't really enough for two couples.
So he started, really.
We decided that I am not going to work for the time being, that I-- because he was really-- both of the children were sick.
I mean, they were-- they did not, physically-- I mean, you know, they couldn't-- the food, the-- the reflex-- they had sort of a certain kind of a reflex, that they-- they ate something.
And they didn't want to eat it.
And they were bringing-- you know.
So they were not really healthy.
Physically, they were not healthy.
It took years, you know.
Now, thanks God, they're as healthy as you can be.
But then, they went were not.
So he started to travel.
And he started to-- he went into small goods.
And he went into small goods.
And he had a big clientele in the country.
And the other things he was helping them too-- with interpretations of letters, with writing letters.
And then he was sending me to do all kinds of things for him, you know, businesswise, and with the letters, and in various languages.
So it evolved like this.
Yes.
We did establish a very-- again, I went-- I was the heart of this family.
And it was very important that-- that the children-- and this my husband wholeheartedly agreed.
It wasn't easy, as he was working on a Saturday, to convince-- how do you say to a young boy who wants to go to shul every Saturday, you know?
How do you explain to him that for him to go to shul-- which he wanted.
And he went to shul.
When we moved here, he was 14.
And he went to shul.
He had to [NON-ENGLISH].
And he said, mom, I just can't understand one thing.
I go to shul.
And I listen to the gentleman talking there.
All they talk about is investments.
And the women talk about fashion.
So he said, all I want to do-- I go there to daven.
I go there for social, too, to meet-- you know, I meet a lot of people.
I didn't know what to answer him.
Because, you see, with us, my father prayed at home.
Or he went to shul.
But I said to him, look, I really-- I don't know what to say.
And I was always-- I don't know any [NON-ENGLISH], you know?
It was an honest, open question.
And I owed him an honest answer.
This is the way what I believed.
So anyhow, I didn't have a real answer for him.
So I just tried to answer sort of tentatively.
But he said-- I asked, later on, somebody.
And she said, what you could have told him-- what, you didn't know that they were [NON-ENGLISH]?
I said, no.
I didn't know that they were [NON-ENGLISH].
I just knew about synagogues.
And she said, well, they were [NON-ENGLISH].
And those people were used to [NON-ENGLISH].
Because in [NON-ENGLISH], you went a few times during the day.
It was just around the corner, or in your own house.
And people were talking in the morning-- who was born, who died, you know-- about business or lack of business, whatever happened.
And this is-- this is why.
And did your religious beliefs change from-- because you said before the war, you were Orthodox.
And did that change?
Well, it slowly, like most of us-- I mean, I was not-- after we-- after the work was done, I mean, I had to work on a Saturday, when we had the milk bar.
But I always felt very guilty.
If I did something on the Shabbat, I always felt very guilty.
But if I had to, I did it.
So you know, we all-- after the war, we are not-- people like myself-- we are trying to find sort of little excuses, you know?
OK, you know, this is-- we are not so strict.
After all, you are not Ultra-Orthodox.
But I try to-- if I do something on a Shabbat, I still sort of feel like, you know, a twinge.
I can literally sort of-- it hurts, you know?
So I still feel like this.
But I buy kosher meat.
And you know, I do sort of-- but I can't say that I really keep, the way my parents kept, and my grandparents.
I wouldn't even, you know-- I wouldn't even compare myself.
But I think that most of the time, I have a clear conscience.
So I think that Judaism, is above-- it's got other meanings.
I don't think I'm 100%.
I pick up the phone on a Saturday, if it rings, you see.
I sort of-- things like this.
So some people, who are really orthodox, they don't, you know.
But I do.
So I know if I do something that I shouldn't.
But some things I still do, nevertheless.
And this evolved.
Because the role models, you know-- we just got a bit lenient with ourselves.
You see, the leniency was not for our family.
So it existed, where the continuity was there.
But with the breaking of the continuity, we allowed certain leniences, yes.
And was your husband Orthodox, as well?
My husband-- if you would ask people that know him, his great-grandfather was one of the-- one of the best-known rabbis in Poland.
His-- his grandfather was a rabbi in the shul.
And his mother was particularly very-- yes.
So he-- he knew all the-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what it is?
I mean, if there were-- if there was a Jewish law that existed, he knew it by heart.
But if I say that he-- he acted.
He had always a little siddur in his car, you see.
He always had things like this.
But sometimes he sort of-- I think he had a inner struggle with it.
Yes.
C-H-R-Z-A-N-O-W-S-K-I.
Chrzanowski, yes.
Yes.
We changed it really when our children started school.
And it was sort of funny that they were called Chrzanowski.
And they were learning to spell.
And the spelling was sort of-- in the beginning, you know, it didn't roll off easily off the tongue.
So we changed it to Crane, which got the same meaning.
